questions.question: Explain dual-mode operation in operating systems. Why is it necessary? (5 marks)
keyPoints: Two modes: User mode (mode bit=1) and Kernel mode (mode bit=0)
Protects OS and hardware from errant user programs
Privileged instructions can only execute in kernel mode
System calls cause transition from user to kernel mode via trap
Hardware support via mode bit ensures enforcement

questions.question: How does the timer mechanism prevent a process from monopolizing the CPU? (3 marks)
keyPoints: OS sets timer (counter + fixed-rate clock) before giving control to user process
Counter decrements at each clock tick; interrupt generated when it reaches zero
Interrupt transfers control back to OS, which can then schedule another process

questions.question: Differentiate between hardware interrupts and software interrupts (traps) with examples. (3 marks)
keyPoints: Hardware interrupts: Generated by external devices (keyboard, disk, timer), asynchronous
Software interrupts (traps): Generated by programs, either intentionally (system calls) or due to errors (divide by zero)
Both cause transfer of control to OS via interrupt vector

questions.question: Describe the complete flow of a system call from user program to kernel and back. (5 marks)
keyPoints: User program places system call number and parameters in registers
Trap instruction executed, hardware switches mode bit to kernel (0)
OS examines system call number, dispatches to appropriate handler
Handler executes requested service in kernel mode
OS places return value, switches mode bit back to user (1), returns control
